# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**What is meant by the "inversion" of sucrose? Why is the hydrolysed product called "invert sugar"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The hydrolysis of sucrose by acid or the enzyme invertase yields an equimolar mixture of D-glucose and D-fructose. **(1 mark)**
- Sucrose is **dextrorotatory** with $[\alpha]_D^{20} = +66.5°$, while the resulting mixture is **levorotatory** with $[\alpha]_D^{20} = -19.85°$ (since fructose has a much stronger negative rotation, $[-92.0°]$, that dominates the weaker positive rotation of glucose, $[+52.7°]$). **(1 mark)**
- Because the sign of optical rotation *inverts* (reverses) during the reaction, the process is called **inversion of sucrose** and the product is termed **invert sugar**. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**List any three factors that affect the rate of acid hydrolysis of sucrose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Concentration / strength of acid (H⁺ ion concentration):** Higher [H⁺] → faster hydrolysis (catalytic role). **(1 mark)**
2. **Temperature:** Rate roughly doubles for every 10 °C rise (Arrhenius dependence). **(1 mark)**
3. **Concentration of sucrose solution:** Higher concentration → more reactant molecules → faster initial rate (though order is pseudo-first-order). **(1 mark)**
*(Bonus acceptable answers: presence of enzyme, surface area for solid substrates like starch, stirring.)*

---

## 5.2 PART-B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Sucrose Hydrolysis by Polarimetry

`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]`

**(a)** Derive the first-order rate expression for the acid-catalysed hydrolysis of sucrose, showing how the optical rotation data is used to determine the rate constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment, a 5 % sucrose solution is hydrolysed using 0.1 N HCl at 30 °C. The following data were recorded using a 2 dm polarimeter tube:
$\alpha_0 = +12.00°,\ \alpha_\infty = -4.00°,\ \alpha_{30\ \text{min}} = +6.50°$.
Calculate: (i) the rate constant $k$, (ii) the half-life $t_{1/2}$, and (iii) the percentage hydrolysis after 30 minutes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Write the rate equation (1 mark):**
$$-\frac{dS}{dt} = k \cdot S \quad \text{(pseudo first order, [H}_2\text{O] is constant)}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from 0 to t (1 mark):**
$$\ln S_0 - \ln S_t = k t \;\Rightarrow\; \ln\!\left(\frac{S_0}{S_t}\right) = k t$$

**Step 3 — Use Biot's Law to link concentration with rotation (2 marks):**
Since $\alpha = [\alpha] \cdot c \cdot l$ and path length $l$ and specific rotation $[\alpha]$ are constants, rotation is directly proportional to concentration. Also at infinite time, the rotation equals $\alpha_\infty$ (corresponding to all product). The amount of *unreacted* sucrose is proportional to $(\alpha_t - \alpha_\infty)$.

**Step 4 — Substitute and obtain the polarimetric equation (2 marks):**
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\alpha_0 - \alpha_\infty}{\alpha_t - \alpha_\infty}\right) = k t \;\Rightarrow\; k = \frac{1}{t} \ln\!\left(\frac{\alpha_0 - \alpha_\infty}{\alpha_t - \alpha_\infty}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Mention verification (1 mark):**
Plotting $\ln(\alpha_t - \alpha_\infty)$ vs $t$ gives a straight line of slope $-k$, confirming first-order kinetics.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Rate constant $k$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{1}{30} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{12.00 - (-4.00)}{6.50 - (-4.00)}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{30} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{16.00}{10.50}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{30} \cdot \ln(1.5238) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{30} \cdot 0.4213 \\[4pt]
\mathbf{k} &= \mathbf{0.01404\ \text{min}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Half-life $t_{1/2}$ (2 marks):**

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{0.01404} = \mathbf{49.36\ \text{minutes}}
$$

**(iii) % Hydrolysis at $t=30$ min (2 marks):**

$$
\%\ \text{Hydrolysis} = \frac{12.00 - 6.50}{12.00 - (-4.00)} \times 100 = \frac{5.50}{16.00} \times 100 = \mathbf{34.38\ \%}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Starch Hydrolysis by Reducing Sugar Method (ALTERNATIVE)

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of the DNS (3,5-Dinitrosalicylic acid) method for determining the extent of hydrolysis of starch. Mention the role of each reagent used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** 10 g of soluble starch was hydrolysed with 100 mL of 1 N HCl for 45 minutes. The hydrolysate was neutralised, made up to 250 mL, and the reducing sugar content was estimated by the DNS method. 1 mL of diluted hydrolysate gave an absorbance of 0.420 at 540 nm, corresponding (from glucose calibration curve) to 0.45 mg/mL glucose. Calculate the **percentage hydrolysis** and the **Dextrose Equivalent (DE)** of the hydrolysate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Principle (3 marks):**
The DNS reagent (3,5-dinitrosalicylic acid + sodium potassium tartrate + NaOH) is yellow in its oxidised form. The **reducing-end sugars** (maltose, glucose) produced during starch hydrolysis reduce the **nitro groups (–NO₂)** of DNS to **amino groups (–NH₂)**, forming a red-brown **3-amino-5-nitrosalicylic acid** complex. The intensity of the colour, measured at **540 nm**, is proportional to the concentration of reducing sugars.

**Role of reagents (4 marks):**
| Reagent | Role | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 3,5-Dinitrosalicylic acid | Oxidising agent; gets reduced by reducing sugars | 1 |
| NaOH | Provides alkaline medium; ionises DNS and helps chromophore formation | 1 |
| Sodium potassium tartrate (Rochelle salt) | Chelates Cu²⁺/prevents aerial oxidation; maintains ionic strength | 1 |
| Heat (boiling water bath 5–10 min) | Accelerates the redox reaction; develops maximum colour | 1 |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** 10 g starch, total volume after hydrolysis = 250 mL, dilution → 1 mL contains 0.45 mg glucose.

**Step 1 — Total reducing sugar in hydrolysate (2 marks):**
$$
\text{Glucose in 1 mL diluted} = 0.45\ \text{mg} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{g}
$$
*(Assuming no further dilution, i.e., dilution factor = 1)*

$$
\text{Total glucose} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 250\ \text{mL} = 0.1125\ \text{g}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical maximum glucose from 10 g starch (2 marks):**

Starch is a polymer of glucose $(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n$. Hydrolysis gives glucose $(C_6H_{12}O_6)$.
- Molar mass of one anhydroglucose unit = 162 g/mol
- Molar mass of glucose = 180 g/mol

$$
\text{Theoretical max glucose} = 10 \times \frac{180}{162} = \mathbf{11.11\ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage hydrolysis (2 marks):**
$$
\%\ \text{Hydrolysis} = \frac{0.1125}{11.11} \times 100 = \mathbf{1.01\ \%}
$$

*(Note: low value because the hydrolysate is highly diluted 250-fold; if the 1 mL was from a 1:10 dilution, the answer would be ~10.1 %.)*

**Step 4 — Dextrose Equivalent (DE) (1 mark):**
$$
DE = \frac{\text{Reducing sugar (as glucose)}}{\text{Total carbohydrate}} \times 100 = \frac{0.1125}{10} \times 100 = \mathbf{1.125\ \%}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — AVOID THESE:**
> 1. **Sign Error in ($\alpha_t - \alpha_\infty$):** Students often write $(\alpha_\infty - \alpha_t)$ instead of $(\alpha_t - \alpha_\infty)$, producing a *negative* $k$. Always keep the form: **$(\alpha_0 - \alpha_\infty) / (\alpha_t - \alpha_\infty)$**.
> 2. **Forgetting that water is in excess:** Failing to state *why* it is pseudo first-order costs 1 full mark.
> 3. **Mixing up units:** Using $k$ in $\text{min}^{-1}$ with $t$ in seconds when computing $t_{1/2}$. Convert everything to **the same time unit**.
> 4. **Specific rotation confusion:** Writing fructose as $+92°$ instead of $-92°$ is a guaranteed 0.5–1 mark loss.
> 5. **In starch problems:** Forgetting to **neutralise** the acid hydrolysate with NaOH before DNS estimation — acidic solution changes the absorbance and gives wrong values.
> 6. **DNS boiling time:** Stopping the reaction at < 5 min gives incomplete colour development; > 15 min may cause charring. Mention **5–10 min in boiling water bath** explicitly.
> 7. **Labelling axis:** In the graph of $\ln(\alpha_t - \alpha_\infty)$ vs $t$, label the **Y-axis correctly as $\ln(\alpha_t - \alpha_\infty)$**, not just $\alpha_t$.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (print & pin to your wall!)**

- ✅ Sucrose hydrolysis = **pseudo first-order** (water in excess), catalysed by **H⁺** or **invertase**.
- ✅ Reaction produces **invert sugar** (1:1 glucose : fructose) — name due to **sign change in optical rotation**.
- ✅ Specific rotations: Sucrose = **+66.5°**; Invert sugar = **−19.85°**; Fructose is the dominant negative rotator at **−92.0°**.
- ✅ Master formula: $k = \dfrac{1}{t}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_0 - \alpha_\infty}{\alpha_t - \alpha_\infty}\right)$.
- ✅ Half-life: $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ — independent of initial concentration (true first-order signature).
- ✅ % Hydrolysis: $\dfrac{\alpha_0 - \alpha_t}{\alpha_0 - \alpha_\infty}\times 100$ (polarimetry) or $\dfrac{\text{Reducing sugar formed}}{\text{Theoretical max}}\times 100$ (DNS).
- ✅ **Graph:** $\ln(\alpha_t - \alpha_\infty)$ vs $t$ is a **straight line with slope = $-k$** — always draw and label this in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Starch hydrolysis** by acid yields a mixture of glucose + maltose + dextrins; **amylase enzyme** yields mainly **maltose**.
- ✅ **DNS method** measures **reducing ends**; chromophore read at **540 nm**; boiled **5–10 min** in alkaline medium.
- ✅ **Iodine test** (KI/I₂) is qualitative: **blue-black** = unhydrolysed starch; **brown/yellow** = hydrolysed (no starch).
- ✅ **Units to remember:** $k$ in $\text{min}^{-1}$ (or $\text{s}^{-1}$), $t_{1/2}$ in min (or s), % hydrolysis dimensionless.
- ✅ **Lab pro tip:** Always **rinse the polarimeter tube** with the solution to be measured (not water) before taking the final reading.
- ✅ **Industrial note:** The **Dextrose Equivalent (DE)** classifies glucose syrups: DE 30 = maltodextrin, DE 42 = standard syrup, DE 95 = high-D-glucose syrup.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acid hydrolysis and action of salivary amylase on starch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL308 - FOOD CHEMISTRY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Acid hydrolysis and action of salivary amylase on starch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Acid Hydrolysis of Starch — Formal Definition

**Acid hydrolysis of starch** is the process of cleaving the glycosidic linkages (primarily the α-1,4 and α-1,6 bonds) in the starch polysaccharide using dilute mineral acid (typically **HCl at 1–3% concentration**) under controlled heat (boiling water bath, 100 °C), yielding progressively smaller fragments — **dextrins → maltose → glucose** — which are reducing sugars detectable by Fehling's / Benedict's / DNS reagents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Hydrolysis is the chemical reaction in which a water molecule is added to a bond, breaking it. In starch, the C–O–C (glycosidic) bonds are cleaved, and the reaction is accelerated by H⁺ ions acting as a catalyst. This is the **basis of all digestive and industrial breakdown of starch**.

### 1.2 Action of Salivary Amylase (Ptyalin) on Starch — Formal Definition

**Salivary amylase** (EC 3.2.1.1), also called **ptyalin**, is a hydrolytic enzyme secreted by the **parotid and submandibular salivary glands** in humans. It catalyzes the random endo-hydrolysis of **α-1,4-glycosidic bonds** in starch (amylose and amylopectin), producing **maltose, maltotriose, and limit dextrins**. The enzyme requires a near-neutral pH (**optimum pH = 6.6–7.0**) and body temperature (**optimum T = 37 °C**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Clarification for KTU Exam:** Salivary amylase **cannot** hydrolyze α-1,6 bonds (branch points in amylopectin). It only cleaves α-1,4 bonds. The action stops in the stomach because gastric HCl denatures the enzyme below pH 3.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Analogy 1 — "Snipping a Long Pearl Necklace":**
Imagine starch as a long pearl necklace where each pearl is a glucose unit. *Acid hydrolysis* is like cutting the thread with scissors at random places — many small pieces of varying lengths come out (dextrins and sugars). *Enzymatic hydrolysis* (amylase) is like a tailor's scissors that **only cut certain types of knots** (α-1,4 links), leaving branch knots (α-1,6) untouched, producing a specific uniform set of pieces (maltose mainly).

**Analogy 2 — "Wood vs. Carpenter":**
A chemical reagent (HCl) is like fire that indiscriminately burns wood. A biological catalyst (amylase) is like a carpenter with a special saw that only cuts at specific joints. The fire is faster but less specific; the carpenter is slower but produces a clean, uniform product.

**Analogy 3 — "Cooking vs. Chewing":**
When you chew bread (containing starch), it tastes slightly sweet after a minute — this is salivary amylase converting starch → maltose (maltose is sweet). When you boil starch with acid, you are doing the same chemically but at industrial scale, producing glucose syrup used in confectionery.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Acid Hydrolysis | Salivary Amylase |
|---|---|---|
| Catalyst | **HCl (2%)** | **Ptyalin (EC 3.2.1.1)** |
| Optimum pH | **< 1.0** | **6.6 – 7.0** |
| Optimum Temperature | **100 °C** | **37 °C** |
| Bond specificity | **α-1,4 & α-1,6 (non-specific)** | **α-1,4 only (specific)** |
| End product | **D-Glucose** | **Maltose + Dextrins** |
| Time required | **30–60 min** | **15–30 min** |
| Detection reagent | **Benedict's / Fehling's** | **Iodine test + Benedict's** |

### 1.5 Why This Experiment Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Relevance:** Acid hydrolysis is used to manufacture **glucose syrup, high-fructose corn syrup (HFCS), and dextrose monohydrate** in the food industry. Salivary amylase studies are foundational to understanding **human nutrition, malting of cereals (beer brewing), bread staling, and infant digestion** (where pancreatic amylase is immature).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chemistry of Starch — Substrate Understanding

Starch is a **polysaccharide of D-glucose** linked by glycosidic bonds, composed of two polymers:

- **Amylose (15–25%)** — Linear, water-soluble, α-1,4 linked glucose units, **M.W. ≈ 10⁵ – 10⁶ Da**
- **Amylopectin (75–85%)** — Branched, water-insoluble, α-1,4 backbone with α-1,6 branch points every 24–30 glucose units, **M.W. ≈ 10⁶ – 10⁸ Da**

The general starch molecule can be represented as:

$$(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5)_n + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+ \text{ or amylase}} n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$$

where **n = Degree of Polymerization (DP)**.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Acid Hydrolysis

Acid hydrolysis proceeds through a **protonation–cleavage** mechanism:

1. **Protonation** of the glycosidic oxygen by H⁺ from the acid.
2. **Cleavage** of the C–O bond, generating a **cyclic oxocarbenium ion** intermediate.
3. **Nucleophilic attack** by water on the oxocarbenium ion.
4. **Release of free glucose** and regeneration of H⁺ (catalyst).

The overall reaction:

$$\text{Starch} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+, \Delta} \text{Dextrins} \rightarrow \text{Maltose} \rightarrow \text{D-Glucose}$$

### 2.3 Mechanism of Salivary Amylase Action

Salivary amylase is an **endo-acting hydrolase** (EC 3.2.1.1) that:

1. **Binds** to the substrate at active sites containing Asp, Glu, and Asp residues (catalytic triad).
2. **Hydrolyzes α-1,4 glycosidic bonds** randomly within the chain (endo-action).
3. **Releases maltose (disaccharide), maltotriose (trisaccharide), and α-limit dextrins** (containing α-1,6 branch points that cannot be cleaved).
4. **Becomes inactive** below pH 3 (denatured in stomach) — explaining why starch digestion resumes in the duodenum via pancreatic amylase.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Starch hydrolysis general equation | $(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5)_n + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow n\,\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$ | — | Stoichiometry |
| 2 | % Hydrolysis of starch | $\%\text{Hydrolysis} = \dfrac{\text{Glucose released (g)}}{\text{Total starch taken (g)} \times 1.11} \times 100$ | % | Acid hydrolysis quantification |
| 3 | Conversion factor (anhydrous glucose from starch) | **1.11** (162/180) | dimensionless | Stoichiometric factor for 1.11 g starch = 1.0 g glucose |
| 4 | Optical rotation of starch | $[\alpha]_D^{20} = +196°$ | degrees | Polarimetry |
| 5 | Iodine complex color (amylose) | Deep **blue** ($\lambda_{max} \approx 620$ nm) | — | Qualitative starch test |
| 6 | Iodine complex color (amylopectin) | Reddish-**purple / violet** ($\lambda_{max} \approx 540$ nm) | — | Branched starch detection |
| 7 | Iodine test for dextrins | **Reddish-brown → No color** as hydrolysis proceeds | — | End-point indicator |
| 8 | Benedict's test (positive) | Brick-red Cu₂O precipitate | — | Reducing sugar detection |
| 9 | DNS test absorbance | Read at **540 nm** | absorbance | Quantitative reducing sugar |
| 10 | Miller's equation for hydrolysis | $\dfrac{1}{DP_t} - \dfrac{1}{DP_0} = k\,t$ | — | First-order kinetics |
| 11 | Rate constant of acid hydrolysis | $k \propto [\text{H}^+]$ | L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ | First-order in H⁺ |
| 12 | Optimum pH of salivary amylase | **6.6 – 7.0** | — | Physiological condition |
| 13 | Optimum temperature | **37 °C** (amylase) / **100 °C** (acid) | °C | Experimental setup |
| 14 | Benedict's titration factor | $0.05$ g glucose / 10 mL Fehling's | g | Volumetric quantification |
| 15 | Reducing sugar from Benedict's | $\text{Reducing sugar (\%)} = \dfrac{T \times D \times 0.05 \times 100}{V \times W}$ | % | Standard formula |

> Where: $T$ = titre value, $D$ = dilution factor, $V$ = aliquot volume, $W$ = sample weight.

### 2.5 Bond Specificity — Critical Distinction for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Enzyme / Reagent | α-1,4 bond | α-1,6 bond |
> |---|---|---|
> | **HCl (acid)** | ✅ Cleaves | ✅ Cleaves (slower) |
> | **Salivary amylase (Ptyalin)** | ✅ Cleaves | ❌ Cannot cleave |
> | **Glucoamylase** | ✅ Cleaves | ✅ Cleaves (slow) |
> | **Debranching enzyme (Pullulanase)** | ❌ | ✅ Specific |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Glucose Syrup Industry** — Acid hydrolysis produces DE (Dextrose Equivalent) 30–42 syrups; enzymatic hydrolysis (using glucoamylase) produces higher DE syrups (DE 95+).
2. **Beer Brewing** — Malted barley's amylase hydrolyzes starch into fermentable maltose during mashing.
3. **Bread-making** — Salivary amylase action during chewing releases maltose; pancreatic amylase continues in the gut.
4. **Starch-based bioplastics** — Controlled acid hydrolysis reduces crystallinity for film formation.
5. **Medical diagnostics** — Serum amylase elevated in acute pancreatitis (normal 30–110 U/L).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the % Hydrolysis Formula

**Problem Setup:**
A student hydrolyzes **1.0 g of soluble starch** in 100 mL of 2% HCl by boiling for 30 minutes. The hydrolysate is neutralized, made to 250 mL, and 25 mL aliquot is titrated against Fehling's solution. The titre value is **20.0 mL** (with 1 mL Fehling's ≡ 0.005 g glucose).

**Step 1: Glucose in the aliquot**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose in 25 mL aliquot} &= \text{Titre (mL)} \times \text{Factor (g/mL)} \\
&= 20.0 \times 0.005 \\
&= 0.100 \text{ g glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Glucose in the total 250 mL hydrolysate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total glucose} &= 0.100 \times \dfrac{250}{25} \\
&= 0.100 \times 10 \\
&= 1.00 \text{ g glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the conversion factor (1.11 g starch → 1.0 g glucose)**

The theoretical maximum glucose obtainable from 1.0 g starch is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical glucose max} &= \text{Starch mass} \times \dfrac{180}{162} \\
&= 1.0 \times 1.11 \\
&= 1.11 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Note:* The factor 1.11 arises because each glucose unit in starch loses 1 H₂O during polymerization. Thus 162 g of anhydrous glucose unit in starch gives 180 g of free glucose upon hydrolysis.

**Step 4: Calculate % hydrolysis**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Hydrolysis} &= \dfrac{\text{Glucose obtained}}{\text{Theoretical max glucose}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1.00}{1.11} \times 100 \\
&= 90.09\% \approx 90.1\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Miller's First-Order Kinetics Equation

The hydrolysis of starch is **pseudo-first-order** at constant acid concentration. If $DP_0$ is the initial degree of polymerization and $DP_t$ at time $t$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{DP_t} - \dfrac{1}{DP_0} = k \cdot t
$$

This equation is used to determine the **rate constant $k$** for acid hydrolysis experiments by plotting $\dfrac{1}{DP_t}$ vs. $t$, which yields a straight line of slope $k$.

**Application example:**
If $DP_0 = 1000$ (native starch) and $DP_t = 100$ at $t = 60$ min:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{100} - \dfrac{1}{1000} &= k \times 60 \\
0.01 - 0.001 &= 60\,k \\
0.009 &= 60\,k \\
k &= 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{min}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Salivary Amylase Activity Calculation (DNS Method)

**Problem Setup:**
A salivary amylase assay is performed using DNS reagent. The standard curve gives glucose equivalent concentrations. Sample absorbance (540 nm) is **0.450** corresponding to **55 μg glucose** in 1 mL reaction mixture. Enzyme used: 0.1 mL saliva diluted 1:100. Reaction time: 5 min at 37 °C.

**Step 1: Calculate enzyme activity (U/mL)**

One unit (U) of amylase = 1 μmol glucose released per minute at 37 °C.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose released} &= 55\,\mu\text{g/mL} \\
\text{In μmol} &= \dfrac{55}{180} = 0.3056\,\mu\text{mol/mL} \\
\text{Per minute} &= \dfrac{0.3056}{5} = 0.0611\,\mu\text{mol mL}^{-1}\text{min}^{-1} \\
\text{Activity per mL saliva} &= 0.0611 \times 100 \text{ (dilution factor)} \\
&= 6.11\,\text{U/mL}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The salivary amylase activity in the original sample = **6.11 U/mL**

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Test

| Test Target | Method | Reagent | Observation | Inference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Starch present | Iodine test | I₂ / KI solution | Blue-black color | Starch intact |
| Starch partially hydrolyzed | Iodine test | I₂ / KI solution | Reddish-brown | Dextrins present |
| Starch fully hydrolyzed | Iodine test | I₂ / KI solution | No color change | Starch absent |
| Reducing sugar absent | Benedict's | Benedict's reagent | Blue (no ppt) | Hydrolysis not started |
| Reducing sugar present | Benedict's | Benedict's reagent | Green → Yellow → Orange → Brick red | Hydrolysis progressing |
| Quantification | DNS / Lane-Eynon | DNS / Fehling's | Absorbance / Titre | Calculate % hydrolysis |

### 3.5 Time Course Table for Acid Hydrolysis (Expected Observations)

| Time (min) | Iodine Test Color | Benedict's Test | % Hydrolysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | **Deep blue** | Blue (no ppt) | 0 % |
| 5 | **Blue-violet** | Faint green | ~10 % |
| 15 | **Reddish-brown** | Yellow-orange | ~40 % |
| 30 | **Light brown** | Brick red | ~70–90 % |
| 60 | **No color** | Brick red (max) | ~95–99 % |
| > 90 | No color | Brick red | Caramelization starts |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Experimental Procedure (Combined)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Prepare 1% starch solution] --> B[Divide into 2 sets: Set A for Acid Hydrolysis, Set B for Salivary Amylase]
    B --> C1[Set A: Add 5 mL of 2% HCl to 50 mL starch soln]
    B --> C2[Set B: Add 5 mL of fresh saliva to 50 mL starch soln]
    C1 --> D1[Boil in water bath at 100 degC]
    C2 --> D2[Incubate at 37 degC in water bath]
    D1 --> E1[Withdraw 5 mL aliquots at 0, 5, 15, 30, 60 min]
    D2 --> E2[Withdraw 5 mL aliquots at 0, 2, 5, 10, 15 min]
    E1 --> F1[Neutralize with Na2CO3]
    E2 --> F2[Add DNS reagent / boil]
    F1 --> G1[Perform Iodine test]
    F2 --> G2[Perform Iodine test]
    G1 --> H1[Perform Benedict test]
    G2 --> H2[Measure absorbance at 540 nm]
    H1 --> I1[Record observations: blue to colorless transition]
    H2 --> I2[Plot glucose concentration vs time]
    I1 --> J1[Calculate percent hydrolysis]
    I2 --> J2[Calculate enzyme activity U per mL]
    J1 --> K[End: Compare acid vs enzyme action]
    J2 --> K
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mechanism of Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Starch Polysaccharide] --> M1[HCl hydrolysis pathway]
    S --> M2[Amylase hydrolysis pathway]
    M1 --> I1[Protonation of glycosidic O]
    I1 --> I2[Cleavage of C-O-C bond]
    I2 --> I3[Oxocarbenium ion intermediate]
    I3 --> I4[Water attack]
    I4 --> P1[Products: Glucose + Dextrins + Maltose]
    M2 --> J1[Enzyme substrate complex formation]
    J1 --> J2[Cleavage of alpha 1,4 bonds only]
    J2 --> J3[Maltose release]
    J3 --> P1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Iodine Test Color Transition Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[0 min: Deep Blue] --> B[5 min: Blue Violet]
    B --> C[15 min: Reddish Brown]
    C --> D[30 min: Light Brown]
    D --> E[60 min: No Color]
    E --> F[End Point of Hydrolysis Achieved]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Salivary Amylase Specificity Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Starch Granule] --> B[Amylose 15 to 25 percent]
    A --> C[Amylopectin 75 to 85 percent]
    B --> D1[Linear alpha 1,4 bonds]
    C --> D2[Alpha 1,4 backbone]
    C --> E[Alpha 1,6 branch points]
    D1 --> F1[Ptyalin cleaves here]
    D2 --> F1
    E --> G[Ptyalin CANNOT cleave]
    F1 --> H1[Maltose + Maltotriose + Limit Dextrins]
    G --> H1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short Answer)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`** *What is salivary amylase? Write its EC number and optimum pH.*

> **Model Answer:** Salivary amylase, also called **ptyalin**, is a hydrolytic enzyme secreted by the salivary glands (parotid and submandibular glands). Its **Enzyme Commission (EC) number is 3.2.1.1**. It catalyzes the endo-hydrolysis of **α-1,4-glycosidic bonds** in starch, producing maltose and dextrins. The **optimum pH is 6.6–7.0**, and optimum temperature is **37 °C** (body temperature).

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`** *Why does iodine test give a blue-black color with starch but no color with glucose?*

> **Model Answer:** The blue-black color arises due to the **helical coiling of amylose chains** (amylose–iodine complex). The iodine molecules (I₃⁻ and I₅⁻) slot into the hydrophobic helical cavity of amylose, forming a **charge-transfer complex** that absorbs visible light at ~620 nm, appearing deep blue-black. Glucose is a **monosaccharide** lacking the helical polymer structure, hence **no complex forms**, and iodine retains its original yellow-brown color.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the procedure for acid hydrolysis of starch. What are the products formed at each stage? How is the end-point determined?

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Starch is hydrolyzed by boiling with dilute mineral acid (HCl). The glycosidic bonds (α-1,4 and α-1,6) are cleaved progressively, producing dextrins, maltose, and finally D-glucose.

**Procedure:**
1. Prepare **100 mL of 1% (w/v) soluble starch solution** in a 250 mL conical flask.
2. Add **5 mL of concentrated HCl (to make ~2% w/v HCl final concentration)**.
3. Place the flask in a **boiling water bath** (100 °C) — this is the zero time of hydrolysis.
4. Withdraw **5 mL aliquots at 0, 5, 10, 15, 30, and 60 minutes** into clean test tubes.
5. **Cool** each aliquot under running tap water.
6. **Neutralize** with Na₂CO₃ solution (test with litmus) to stop hydrolysis.
7. Perform **iodine test** on each aliquot (1 drop of iodine reagent).
8. Perform **Benedict's test** (or Fehling's) for reducing sugars.

**Products formed at each stage:**

$$
\text{Starch} \rightarrow \text{Amylodextrins (blue with I}_2\text{)} \rightarrow \text{Erythrodextrins (red-brown)} \rightarrow \text{Achrodextrins (no color)} \rightarrow \text{Maltose} \rightarrow \text{D-Glucose}
$$

**End-point determination:** Hydrolysis is considered complete when:
- **Iodine test gives NO color change** (i.e., no starch or dextrins remain).
- **Benedict's test gives maximum brick-red precipitate** (i.e., maximum reducing sugar).

**(b) [7 Marks]** During an acid hydrolysis experiment, 1.0 g starch in 100 mL of 2% HCl was boiled for 30 min, neutralized, and diluted to 250 mL. A 25 mL aliquot required 18.5 mL of Fehling's solution for complete reduction. Given 1 mL Fehling's ≡ 0.005 g glucose, calculate the % hydrolysis. Take 1.11 g starch ≡ 1.0 g glucose.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Glucose in 25 mL aliquot**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose in aliquot} &= 18.5 \times 0.005 \\
&= 0.0925 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total glucose in 250 mL hydrolysate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total glucose} &= 0.0925 \times \dfrac{250}{25} \\
&= 0.0925 \times 10 \\
&= 0.925 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Theoretical max glucose from 1.0 g starch**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical glucose} &= 1.0 \times \dfrac{1.0}{1.11} \\
&= 0.9009 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Note:* Since 1.11 g starch gives 1.0 g glucose, 1.0 g starch will give 1.0/1.11 = 0.9009 g glucose theoretically.

**Step 4: % Hydrolysis**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Hydrolysis} &= \dfrac{0.925}{0.9009} \times 100 \\
&= 102.7\% \approx 100\% \text{ (essentially complete)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Note:* Values >100% indicate either experimental error, presence of free reducing impurities in the starch, or excessive volume changes. The answer is reported as ~100% indicating complete hydrolysis.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the action of salivary amylase on starch. Discuss the effect of pH and temperature on its activity with a neat labeled diagram of the reaction.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Salivary amylase (ptyalin) is a **carbohydrase** that hydrolyzes the **α-1,4-glycosidic bonds** in starch, producing maltose, maltotriose, and limit dextrins. The activity is monitored by:
- **Iodine test** (loss of blue color)
- **Benedict's / DNS test** (increase in reducing sugar)

**Procedure:**
1. Collect **5–10 mL of fresh saliva** in a beaker and dilute 1:1 with distilled water.
2. Prepare **100 mL of 1% starch solution**, boil and cool to 37 °C.
3. Mix **50 mL starch solution + 5 mL saliva + 1 mL toluene** (toluene prevents microbial growth).
4. Incubate at **37 °C**.
5. Withdraw **5 mL aliquots at 0, 2, 5, 10, and 15 min**.
6. Test each aliquot for iodine color and Benedict's reduction.
7. Plot graph of iodine color intensity vs. time.

**Effect of pH:**
- **Optimum pH = 6.6–7.0** (neutral).
- At **pH < 3** (stomach acidity), the enzyme is **denatured** — activity lost permanently.
- At **pH > 9**, the enzyme is also inactivated due to ionization of active site residues.

**Effect of Temperature:**
- **Optimum T = 37 °C** (body temperature).
- At **0 °C**, activity is minimal (low kinetic energy).
- At **> 60 °C**, the enzyme is **thermally denatured** (irreversible loss of 3D structure).
- This is why cooking starch is more digestible — the swollen starch granules are more accessible to amylase.

**Reaction equation:**

$$
\text{Starch} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Ptyalin, pH 6.7, 37 °C}} \text{Maltose} + \text{Maltotriose} + \text{Limit dextrins}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 0.1 mL sample of saliva (diluted 1:50) was incubated with 0.9 mL of 1% starch for 5 min at 37 °C. The reaction was stopped, and DNS reagent was added. Absorbance at 540 nm was 0.380, corresponding (from standard curve) to 45 μg glucose/mL. Calculate the amylase activity in U/mL of original saliva. (1 U = 1 μmol glucose released/min)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Glucose in reaction mixture (per mL)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose released} &= 45\,\mu\text{g/mL} \\
\text{In μmol/mL} &= \dfrac{45}{180} = 0.25\,\mu\text{mol/mL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Per minute basis**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rate} &= \dfrac{0.25}{5\,\text{min}} = 0.05\,\mu\text{mol mL}^{-1}\text{min}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Correct for dilution**

The reaction mixture already represents the diluted saliva (1:50), so the activity in original saliva:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Activity} &= 0.05 \times 50 \\
&= 2.5\,\text{U/mL}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The amylase activity in the original saliva sample = **2.5 U/mL**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.11 conversion factor** — Students often divide directly by 1.0 g starch, leading to >100% hydrolysis values. Always remember **1.11 g starch ≡ 1.0 g glucose**.
> 2. **Confusing salivary and pancreatic amylase** — Salivary amylase is inactivated in the stomach (pH < 3); pancreatic amylase (pH 7.1) continues digestion in the duodenum.
> 3. **Stating "amylase hydrolyzes α-1,6 bonds"** — Wrong. Ptyalin **cannot** cleave α-1,6 bonds; only α-1,4. This distinction is a **favourite 2-mark KTU question**.
> 4. **Using Benedict's test on unhydrolyzed starch** — Starch is a non-reducing polysaccharide; only after hydrolysis to maltose/glucose will Benedict's test give a positive result.
> 5. **Wrong units in enzyme activity** — Activity must be in **U/mL** (μmol/min/mL), not μg/min.
> 6. **Drawing the diagram without arrows** — In KTU ESE, mechanism diagrams **must show arrows for electron flow or proton transfer**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU 2024):**
>
> ✅ Starch = amylose (linear) + amylopectin (branched); both are α-glucans.
> ✅ Acid hydrolysis uses **2% HCl, 100 °C**, non-specific cleavage → final product = **D-glucose**.
> ✅ Salivary amylase (ptyalin) uses **pH 6.6–7.0, 37 °C**, specific for **α-1,4** bonds → final products = **maltose + limit dextrins**.
> ✅ Iodine test: **blue-black (amylose), purple (amylopectin), no color (hydrolysed)**.
> ✅ Benedict's test: **brick-red Cu₂O** = reducing sugar present.
> ✅ Conversion factor **1.11** = mass of starch needed to give 1.0 g free glucose.
> ✅ DNS method measured at **540 nm**; Benedict's volumetric method uses **0.05 g/10 mL** titre factor.
> ✅ Miller's first-order equation: $\dfrac{1}{DP_t} - \dfrac{1}{DP_0} = k \cdot t$
> ✅ Amylase activity unit: **1 U = 1 μmol glucose released per minute at 37 °C**.
> ✅ Ptyalin is **denatured at pH < 3** (stomach) — digestion resumes in duodenum via pancreatic amylase.
> ✅ Industrial acid hydrolysis gives **glucose syrup (DE 30–42)**; enzymatic gives **high DE syrup (DE 95+)**.
> ✅ % Hydrolysis formula = (Experimental glucose / Theoretical glucose) × 100.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of gluten in wheat
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL308 - FOOD CHEMISTRY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of gluten in wheat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Gluten** is an insoluble, viscoelastic protein complex formed when wheat flour is hydrated and mechanically worked (kneaded/washed). It constitutes approximately **80% of the total protein** content of wheat endosperm and is composed of two principal protein fractions:

| Protein Fraction | Classical Classification | Solubility Property | Functional Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gliadin** | Prolamin | Soluble in 70% ethanol | Imparts **extensibility** and viscosity (flow) |
| **Glutenin** | Glutelin | Insoluble in 70% ethanol, soluble in dilute acid/alkali | Imparts **elasticity** and strength (resistance) |

The interaction between gliadin and glutenin, held together by intermolecular **disulfide bonds (S–S)** and hydrogen bonds, produces a continuous three-dimensional protein matrix that traps starch granules and gas during dough fermentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** Estimation of gluten is performed by the *Wet Gluten Method* (Hand-Washing / Glutomatic Method). The principle relies on the fact that gluten proteins are **insoluble in water**, while starch, soluble sugars, and water-soluble proteins (albumins and globulins) get washed away.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wet Gluten** = freshly washed, hydrated gluten mass (contains water).
> **Dry Gluten** = oven-dried gluten mass.
> **Gluten Index** = ratio of gluten retained on a sieve after centrifugation to total wet gluten — an indicator of gluten *quality* (strength vs. weakness).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Rubber Balloon Inside a Sandbag"

Imagine building a tiny **balloon** from a rubber sheet. When you pump air inside, the balloon stretches but does not break — this is *glutenin* (elasticity). Now smear that balloon with **honey** so it slowly oozes and flows — this is *gliadin* (extensibility). Together, gliadin + glutenin behave like a **balloon covered in honey**: elastic enough to hold shape, fluid enough to expand.

Now pack this balloon inside a **bag of wet sand** (the starch granules + water). When you wash the bag with running water, the sand washes away and the balloon emerges intact. What remains in your hand is the gluten — a pale, yellowish, rubbery mass. **The gluten "balloon" held together all the structure, while the soluble "sand" got washed off.**

This is exactly what happens in the laboratory when you knead wheat flour dough under a thin stream of water and squeeze out the starch milk — the remaining sticky, elastic mass is **gluten**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Gluten Form Only in Wheat?

Not all cereals form gluten. Wheat is unique because its storage proteins (prolamins) are exceptionally rich in the amino acid **proline** and contain repetitive peptide domains that favour inter-chain hydrogen bonding. Barley (hordein), rye (secalin) and oats (avenin) form weaker or different protein networks. **Maize and rice lack true gluten-forming proteins.**

> [!NOTE]
> **King George V Standard:** The minimum wet gluten content for good bread-making wheat flour is **≥ 28%**, while biscuit (cookie) flour requires **20–25%**.

## 2.2 Principle of the Experiment

When wheat flour is mixed with water (typically in a **2:1 flour to water ratio** by weight) and kneaded:

1. Hydration causes gluten proteins to swell and unfold.
2. Mechanical work aligns protein molecules along shear lines.
3. Inter-protein disulfide bonds form a continuous network.
4. Repeated washing with water removes:
   - Starch granules (suspension washed off as *starch milk*)
   - Soluble proteins — albumins and globulins
   - Soluble sugars and minerals
   - Bran and husk particles (if whole wheat is used)

The residue is **crude wet gluten**, which is then weighed to quantify the gluten yield per 100 g of flour.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wet Gluten Content (WGC) | $\text{WGC} \left(\%\right) = \dfrac{m_1}{m_0} \times 100$ | %, w/w | $m_1$ = mass of wet gluten (g); $m_0$ = mass of flour taken (g) |
| Dry Gluten Content (DGC) | $\text{DGC} \left(\%\right) = \dfrac{m_2}{m_0} \times 100$ | %, w/w | $m_2$ = mass of oven-dried gluten (g) |
| Gluten Hydration (H) | $H \left(\%\right) = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_2} \times 100$ | % | Indicator of water-holding capacity |
| Wet → Dry Conversion Factor | $\text{DGC} \approx \text{WGC} \times 0.35$ | dimensionless | Empirical; valid for normal wheat |
| Gluten Index (GI) | $\text{GI} = \dfrac{m_{\text{sieve}}}{m_1} \times 100$ | % | Strength indicator; $m_{\text{sieve}}$ = gluten retained on sieve after centrifugation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** All weighings must be done to an accuracy of $\pm 0.01$ g. Wet gluten must be weighed **within 10 minutes** of washing to avoid drying losses.

## 2.4 Factors Influencing Gluten Yield and Quality

- **Wheat Variety:** Hard wheat → higher quantity, stronger gluten. Soft wheat → lower quantity, weaker gluten.
- **Protein Content:** Linear relationship with gluten yield up to ~14% flour protein.
- **pH of Wash Water:** Optimum at **pH 6.5–7.0**; acidic conditions weaken gluten.
- **Temperature of Wash Water:** Maintained at **20 ± 2 °C** (warm water softens and denatures gluten).
- **Salt Addition:** 2% NaCl strengthens gluten by tightening the protein matrix.
- **Ascorbic Acid (Vitamin C):** Strengthens gluten via SH $\rightarrow$ S–S bond formation.
- **Mechanical Over-kneading:** Can rupture disulfide bonds → sticky, weak gluten.

## 2.5 Industrial & Engineering Utility

- **Flour Mill Quality Control:** Segregating bread-flour from biscuit-flour lots.
- **Bakery Industry:** Predicting dough-handling behaviour and final loaf volume.
- **Gluten Extraction Industry:** Vital wheat gluten (VWG) is a major export commodity (~$1.2 billion global market) used as a protein supplement in meat analogs, bakery, and pet food.
- **Celiac Disease Diagnostics:** Quantifying residual gluten in "gluten-free" labelled products (threshold: **< 20 mg/kg** by Codex Alimentarius).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Apparatus & Reagents Required

**Apparatus:**
- Analytical balance (accuracy $\pm 0.01$ g)
- Porcelain or stainless-steel mortar / kneading tray
- Beaker (250 mL)
- Glass stirring rod
- Fine muslin cloth or 88-micron sieve (ISO Standard)
- Hot-air oven (set at 130 ± 2 °C)
- Desiccator
- Stopwatch / timer

**Reagents:**
- Wheat flour (sample)
- Distilled water at **20 ± 2 °C**
- 2% NaCl solution (optional, to strengthen gluten)

## 3.2 Standard Procedure (ICC Standard No. 106 / AACC 38-12)

> [!NOTE]
> The following 10-step protocol is the **manual hand-washing method** prescribed by the International Association for Cereal Science and Technology (ICC).

**Step 1 — Sample Weighing:**
Accurately weigh about **25 g (or 10 g, depending on protocol)** of wheat flour ($m_0$) on a butter paper and transfer to a clean porcelain mortar.

**Step 2 — Dough Formation:**
Add approximately **12–13 mL of distilled water** (at 20 °C) to the flour — a flour:water ratio of roughly **2:1**. Mix with a glass rod to form a stiff, uniform dough ball. Knead gently for **1–2 minutes** until smooth and non-sticky.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dough must be **stiff**, not soft. Excess water hydrates starch and creates a sticky mass that loses starch slowly.

**Step 3 — Rest Period (Optional but Recommended):**
Cover the dough with a wet muslin cloth and let it rest for **20 minutes** at room temperature. This allows complete hydration of the gluten proteins.

**Step 4 — First Wash:**
Hold the dough ball under a thin, gentle stream of tap water (or work it in a bowl with frequent water changes). Squeeze and knead continuously with fingertips. The cloudy white liquid that runs off is **starch milk**.

**Step 5 — Repetitive Washing:**
Continue washing until the runoff water becomes **perfectly clear** (free of starch particles). This usually requires **8–10 minutes** of continuous kneading.

**Step 6 — Indicator Test:**
Add a few drops of the wash water to a beaker of clear water. If no milky cloudiness appears, washing is complete. Alternatively, test with **dilute iodine solution** — absence of **blue-black colour** confirms starch removal.

**Step 7 — Squeeze Out Excess Water:**
Gently press the gluten ball between folds of muslin cloth or between the palms to express excess water. Do **not** stretch or deform aggressively.

**Step 8 — Weigh Wet Gluten:**
Immediately weigh the gluten mass on a tared watch glass ($m_1$). Record the value.

**Step 9 — Oven Drying:**
Transfer the wet gluten to a pre-weighed moisture dish and dry in a hot-air oven at **130 ± 2 °C for 2 hours** (or until constant mass at 105 °C for 4 hours).

**Step 10 — Weigh Dry Gluten:**
Cool the dried gluten in a desiccator for **15 minutes**, then weigh ($m_2$).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Calculation)

**Given:**
- Mass of flour taken: $m_0 = 25.00$ g
- Mass of wet gluten: $m_1 = 8.75$ g
- Mass of dry gluten: $m_2 = 3.12$ g

**Step (a) — Wet Gluten Content:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{WGC} \left(\%\right) &= \frac{m_1}{m_0} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{8.75 \text{ g}}{25.00 \text{ g}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 35.00 \,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (b) — Dry Gluten Content:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DGC} \left(\%\right) &= \frac{m_2}{m_0} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.12 \text{ g}}{25.00 \text{ g}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 12.48 \,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (c) — Gluten Hydration (Water-Holding Capacity):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H \left(\%\right) &= \frac{m_1 - m_2}{m_2} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{8.75 - 3.12}{3.12} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{5.63}{3.12} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 180.45 \,\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (d) — Verification with Empirical Conversion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DGC}_{\text{empirical}} &= \text{WGC} \times 0.35 \\[6pt]
&= 35.00 \times 0.35 \\[6pt]
&= 12.25 \,\%
\end{aligned}$$

Experimental value **12.48 %** vs. empirical **12.25 %** → deviation of **+0.23 %**, well within accepted laboratory tolerance of $\pm 1.5\%$. ✅ The result confirms a high-quality **bread-grade flour**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Flour Type | WGC Range (%) | DGC Range (%) | Hydration (%) | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hard bread wheat | 30 – 40 | 11 – 14 | 180 – 220 | Bread, chapati |
| Soft biscuit wheat | 18 – 26 | 7 – 9 | 160 – 190 | Biscuits, cakes |
| Durum wheat | 32 – 45 | 12 – 16 | 170 – 210 | Pasta, semolina |
| Defatted wheat flour | 38 – 50 | 14 – 18 | 160 – 200 | Gluten extraction industry |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
